subtopic_detail: Cluster headache - acute and prophylactic management
stem: A 35-year-old man presents with a 2-week history of severe unilateral left periorbital headaches occurring 2 to 3 times daily, each lasting 60 to 90 minutes. The headaches wake him at night at approximately the same time. During attacks he paces the room and cannot sit still. He reports left-sided lacrimation, conjunctival injection, nasal congestion, and partial ptosis during the attacks. Between attacks he is completely well. An MRI brain is normal.

data_table.Examination: Between attacks: normal neurological examination, no Horner syndrome

lead_in: What is the most appropriate acute treatment for individual attacks?

options.text: High-flow oxygen at 12-15 L/min via non-rebreather mask
options.rationale: High-flow oxygen (100% at 12-15 L/min via non-rebreather mask for 15-20 minutes) is the first-line acute treatment for cluster headache attacks. It aborts attacks in approximately 78% of patients within 15 minutes with no significant side effects. Subcutaneous sumatriptan 6 mg is the alternative first-line option. Both are superior to oral triptans, which are too slow in onset for cluster headache. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Neurology)

options.text: Oral sumatriptan 100 mg taken at the onset of each headache
options.rationale: Oral sumatriptan is too slow in onset (30-45 minutes) to effectively treat cluster headache attacks, which peak rapidly and last 15-180 minutes. By the time oral sumatriptan reaches therapeutic levels, the attack may have already resolved spontaneously. Subcutaneous sumatriptan (6 mg) or intranasal sumatriptan (20 mg) have faster onset and are effective. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Neurology)

options.text: Oral prednisolone 60 mg daily as a short-term bridging course
options.rationale: A short course of prednisolone (60-80 mg tapering over 2-3 weeks) is used as transitional (bridging) therapy while a preventive agent (verapamil) takes effect. It reduces attack frequency but is not used for aborting individual acute attacks. It bridges the gap between diagnosis and effective prophylaxis. (Source: NICE CG150: Headache)

options.text: Oral paracetamol 1 g combined with ibuprofen 400 mg together
options.rationale: Simple and combination analgesics are ineffective for cluster headache. The severe intensity and short duration of attacks mean that oral analgesics cannot reach therapeutic levels in time, and the pain is not responsive to the mechanisms of paracetamol or NSAIDs. Patients often present having already failed these medications. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Neurology)

options.text: Intranasal lignocaine 10% spray into the ipsilateral nostril
options.rationale: Intranasal lignocaine can provide some relief in cluster headache by anaesthetising the sphenopalatine ganglion and is sometimes used as a third-line option. However, evidence is limited and it is significantly less effective than high-flow oxygen or subcutaneous sumatriptan. It is not recommended as first-line acute treatment. (Source: NICE CG150: Headache)

explanation.summary: Cluster headache is a trigeminal autonomic cephalalgia characterised by severe unilateral periorbital pain with ipsilateral autonomic features (lacrimation, conjunctival injection, nasal congestion, ptosis, miosis). Attacks last 15-180 minutes and occur in clusters lasting weeks to months. Acute treatment is high-flow oxygen or subcutaneous sumatriptan. Prophylaxis with verapamil (first-line) is commenced during a cluster bout, with short-course prednisolone as bridging therapy. Oral triptans and simple analgesics are ineffective.
key_points: High-flow oxygen 12-15 L/min is first-line acute treatment for cluster headache
Subcutaneous sumatriptan 6 mg is the alternative first-line acute treatment
Oral triptans are too slow in onset to abort cluster headache attacks
Verapamil is the first-line prophylactic agent for cluster headache
Prednisolone is used as short-term bridging therapy while verapamil takes effect
explanation.pearls: Cluster headache patients characteristically pace the room and are agitated during attacks - this restless behaviour distinguishes them from migraine patients who prefer to lie still in a dark room, and is a useful bedside diagnostic clue.

stem: A 32-year-old woman of African descent presents with a 6-week history of dry cough, fatigue, and bilateral ankle swelling. She also reports painful raised red lesions on her shins. A chest X-ray shows bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy with pulmonary infiltrates. Serum ACE level is elevated. A biopsy of a skin lesion shows non-caseating granulomas. Spirometry shows a mild restrictive pattern with an FVC of 72% predicted.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 82, BP 118/74 mmHg, RR 16, SpO2 96% RA, Temp 37.0
data_table.Investigations: FBC normal; UEC: eGFR >90, calcium 2.78 mmol/L (ref 2.15-2.55); ACE 98 U/L (ref 8-52); 24-hour urine calcium elevated; LFTs: ALP 145 U/L; spirometry: FVC 72%, FEV1 70%, FEV1/FVC 0.82; DLCO 65% predicted

lead_in: What is the most appropriate treatment?

options.text: Observation only as sarcoidosis is usually self-limiting
options.rationale: While pulmonary sarcoidosis with bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy alone (Scadding stage I-II) often resolves spontaneously, this patient has treatment indications: hypercalcaemia (2.78 mmol/L), reduced DLCO (65%), reduced FVC (72%), and symptomatic disease. Observation would be appropriate only for asymptomatic stage I disease without organ dysfunction. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Respiratory)

options.text: Commence oral prednisolone 0.5 mg/kg/day for 4 to 6 weeks
options.rationale: Oral corticosteroids are first-line treatment for sarcoidosis when treatment is indicated. Indications include symptomatic pulmonary disease with reduced lung function, hypercalcaemia, and significant extrapulmonary involvement. Starting dose is typically prednisolone 0.5 mg/kg/day (approximately 30 mg/day) for 4-6 weeks, then gradual taper over 6-12 months guided by symptoms and lung function. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Respiratory)

options.text: Start methotrexate 15 mg weekly as a steroid-sparing first line
options.rationale: Methotrexate is an effective steroid-sparing agent in sarcoidosis but is second-line, used when corticosteroids cannot be tapered adequately or when steroid side effects are unacceptable. First-line treatment for newly diagnosed sarcoidosis requiring therapy is oral prednisolone. Methotrexate may be added after 3-6 months if steroid tapering proves difficult. (Source: BTS: Sarcoidosis Guidelines)

options.text: Prescribe inhaled budesonide 800 mcg BD for pulmonary disease
options.rationale: Inhaled corticosteroids have limited evidence in pulmonary sarcoidosis. They may provide modest benefit for cough as a symptom but do not adequately treat the granulomatous inflammation, improve lung function, or address the hypercalcaemia. Systemic corticosteroids are needed for the degree of disease in this patient. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Respiratory)

options.text: Commence hydroxychloroquine 200 mg BD for skin and joint disease
options.rationale: Hydroxychloroquine is useful for cutaneous sarcoidosis and arthralgias but does not adequately treat pulmonary sarcoidosis or hypercalcaemia. This patient has significant pulmonary involvement (reduced FVC and DLCO) and hypercalcaemia that require systemic corticosteroids. Hydroxychloroquine may be considered as an adjunct for the erythema nodosum specifically. (Source: BTS: Sarcoidosis Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Sarcoidosis is a multisystem granulomatous disease. Treatment indications include symptomatic pulmonary disease with impaired lung function, hypercalcaemia, cardiac involvement, neurological involvement, and significant eye or skin disease not responding to topical therapy. Oral corticosteroids are first-line, with steroid-sparing agents (methotrexate, azathioprine, mycophenolate) added for refractory disease or intolerable steroid side effects. Erythema nodosum in sarcoidosis is part of Lofgren syndrome and may respond to NSAIDs alone, but this patient has additional treatment indications.
key_points: Oral prednisolone is first-line treatment when sarcoidosis treatment is indicated
Treatment indications: symptomatic lung disease with reduced FVC/DLCO, hypercalcaemia, cardiac/neuro involvement
Hypercalcaemia in sarcoidosis is due to 1-alpha-hydroxylase activity in granulomas converting 25-OH vitamin D to calcitriol
Steroid-sparing agents (methotrexate, azathioprine) are second-line for refractory disease
Asymptomatic bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy (stage I) without organ dysfunction can be observed
explanation.pearls: Sarcoidosis-related hypercalcaemia is driven by unregulated 1-alpha-hydroxylase in granulomas - it responds rapidly to corticosteroids and patients should avoid vitamin D supplementation and excessive sun exposure during active disease.

stem: A 68-year-old man presents with a 12-month history of progressive exertional dyspnoea and a dry cough. He is a retired builder with 20 years of asbestos exposure. He has never smoked. Examination reveals bilateral fine inspiratory crackles at the lung bases and finger clubbing. High-resolution CT shows bilateral basal-predominant honeycombing with traction bronchiectasis and minimal ground glass opacity. Autoimmune serology is negative.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 82, BP 128/76 mmHg, RR 18, SpO2 92% RA at rest
data_table.Investigations: Spirometry: FVC 58% predicted, FEV1 62% predicted, FEV1/FVC 0.86; DLCO 38% predicted; HRCT: bilateral basal honeycombing, traction bronchiectasis, minimal GGO, subpleural distribution

lead_in: What is the most appropriate pharmacological management?

options.text: Oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg/day tapering over three to six months
options.rationale: Corticosteroids are not effective for idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis (or usual interstitial pneumonia pattern) and may cause harm. The PANTHER trial showed that corticosteroids combined with azathioprine and N-acetylcysteine increased mortality in IPF. Corticosteroids are indicated for inflammatory ILDs (sarcoidosis, hypersensitivity pneumonitis, NSIP) but not for established fibrosis with honeycombing. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Respiratory)

options.text: Azathioprine 2 mg/kg/day as immunosuppressive maintenance therapy
options.rationale: Immunosuppression with azathioprine is used for autoimmune-related ILDs (connective tissue disease-associated ILD, sarcoidosis) but is contraindicated in IPF. The PANTHER trial demonstrated increased mortality with the combination of prednisolone, azathioprine, and N-acetylcysteine in IPF. This patient's autoimmune serology is negative. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Respiratory)

options.text: Commence pirfenidone or nintedanib as an antifibrotic agent
options.rationale: The UIP (usual interstitial pneumonia) pattern on HRCT with honeycombing, traction bronchiectasis, basal predominance, and negative autoimmune serology is diagnostic of IPF (or possibly asbestosis given the occupational history, but managed identically). Antifibrotic agents (pirfenidone or nintedanib) slow the rate of FVC decline by approximately 50% and are the standard of care. Both are PBS-listed in Australia for IPF with FVC 50-80% predicted. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Respiratory)

options.text: Commence sildenafil for secondary pulmonary hypertension
options.rationale: Pulmonary hypertension can complicate advanced IPF, and sildenafil has been studied in this context. However, the STEP-IPF trial showed no significant benefit of sildenafil for IPF-associated pulmonary hypertension, and it is not recommended as primary treatment. Antifibrotic therapy to slow disease progression is the priority. (Source: NICE NG147: IPF)

options.text: Prescribe N-acetylcysteine 600 mg TDS as an antioxidant therapy
options.rationale: N-acetylcysteine (NAC) was previously used in IPF based on its antioxidant properties. However, the PANTHER trial showed no benefit of NAC monotherapy in IPF, and the combination arm (NAC + prednisolone + azathioprine) showed harm. NAC is no longer recommended for IPF treatment. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Respiratory)

explanation.summary: Idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis is a progressive fibrosing interstitial lung disease with a median survival of 3-5 years from diagnosis. The UIP pattern on HRCT (basal honeycombing, traction bronchiectasis, minimal ground glass opacity) is diagnostic when clinical context is consistent and autoimmune serology is negative. Antifibrotic agents (pirfenidone and nintedanib) are the standard of care and slow FVC decline. Corticosteroids and immunosuppressants are harmful in IPF. Lung transplantation should be considered for eligible patients.
key_points: UIP pattern on HRCT: basal honeycombing, traction bronchiectasis, subpleural distribution, minimal GGO
Antifibrotic agents (pirfenidone or nintedanib) slow FVC decline by approximately 50%
Corticosteroids and immunosuppressants are harmful in IPF (PANTHER trial)
PBS eligibility for antifibrotics requires FVC 50-80% predicted in Australia
Lung transplantation referral should be considered early in the disease course
explanation.pearls: The DLCO is often the earliest and most disproportionately reduced lung function parameter in IPF - a DLCO below 40% predicted identifies patients at highest risk of mortality and should prompt urgent transplant referral.

stem: A 38-year-old woman presents to her GP with migraine headaches occurring 10 days per month for the past 6 months, significantly impacting her work. She uses sumatriptan for acute attacks, which provides relief but she is using it 3 to 4 times per week. She has a history of well-controlled asthma on a low-dose inhaled corticosteroid. Her BMI is 22 and blood pressure is normal. She is planning pregnancy in the next 12 months.

data_table.Medications: Sumatriptan 50 mg PRN (3-4 times weekly), fluticasone 100 mcg BD inhaled

lead_in: What is the most appropriate prophylactic medication?

options.text: Propranolol 40 mg BD titrated up to effect over four weeks
options.rationale: Propranolol is a first-line migraine prophylactic and is effective. However, it is contraindicated in this patient with asthma - beta-blockers can precipitate bronchospasm in asthmatic patients. Even cardioselective beta-blockers carry some risk. An alternative first-line agent should be chosen. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Neurology)

options.text: Topiramate 25 mg daily titrated up to 50 to 100 mg daily
options.rationale: Topiramate is an effective migraine prophylactic. However, it is teratogenic (FDA category D) and absolutely contraindicated in women planning pregnancy. It is associated with increased risk of cleft lip and palate, and requires reliable contraception during use. Since this patient is planning pregnancy within 12 months, topiramate is not appropriate. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Neurology)

options.text: Sodium valproate 200 mg BD titrated up to effect over weeks
options.rationale: Sodium valproate is effective for migraine prophylaxis but is a known teratogen with significant risks of neural tube defects and neurodevelopmental impairment. It is contraindicated in women of childbearing potential planning pregnancy. Australian guidelines recommend avoiding valproate in any woman who could become pregnant. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Neurology)

options.text: Amitriptyline 10 mg nocte titrated up to 25 to 50 mg nocte
options.rationale: Amitriptyline is a first-line migraine prophylactic that is safe in asthma (unlike propranolol) and has a relatively favourable safety profile in pregnancy (category C - used when benefits outweigh risks, with extensive clinical experience). Starting dose is 10 mg nocte, titrating to 25-50 mg. It is also useful if there is comorbid tension-type headache or insomnia. The frequency of 10 headache days per month and triptan overuse both indicate prophylaxis is needed. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Neurology)

options.text: Commence monthly galcanezumab CGRP monoclonal antibody injection
options.rationale: CGRP monoclonal antibodies (galcanezumab, fremanezumab, erenumab) are effective for migraine prophylaxis and are PBS-listed in Australia for chronic migraine (>=15 headache days/month) after failing two prior prophylactics. This patient has episodic migraine (10 days/month) and has not yet trialled first-line prophylaxis. CGRP antibodies are also not recommended in pregnancy. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Neurology)

explanation.summary: Migraine prophylaxis is indicated when attacks occur 4 or more days per month, significantly impact function, or when acute medication is overused (triptans >10 days/month, simple analgesics >15 days/month). First-line prophylactics include propranolol, amitriptyline, topiramate, and candesartan. The choice is guided by comorbidities and contraindications. In women planning pregnancy, topiramate and valproate are contraindicated. In asthma, beta-blockers are contraindicated. Amitriptyline is the best option when both constraints apply.
key_points: Migraine prophylaxis is indicated for 4+ headache days/month or acute medication overuse
Propranolol is contraindicated in asthma
Topiramate and valproate are contraindicated in women planning pregnancy
Amitriptyline 10-50 mg nocte is safe in asthma and has acceptable pregnancy safety profile
Triptan use >10 days/month suggests medication overuse headache - prophylaxis helps reduce acute medication use
explanation.pearls: Medication overuse headache should be suspected when acute migraine treatments are used more than 10 days per month for triptans or 15 days per month for simple analgesics - starting prophylaxis allows reduction of acute medication use, which itself improves headache frequency.

subtopic_detail: Large vessel occlusion stroke - thrombectomy eligibility
stem: A 72-year-old woman with atrial fibrillation (not on anticoagulation) presents to a regional emergency department 2 hours after sudden onset of right-sided hemiplegia, global aphasia, and forced left gaze deviation. Her NIHSS score is 18. A non-contrast CT brain shows no haemorrhage. CT angiography demonstrates a left middle cerebral artery M1 segment occlusion. CT perfusion shows a small core infarct volume (15 mL) with a large penumbra (120 mL mismatch). The nearest comprehensive stroke centre with endovascular capability is 90 minutes away by road.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 88 (irregular), BP 172/94 mmHg, SpO2 96% RA, Temp 36.9, GCS 10
data_table.Investigations: BSL 8.2 mmol/L; INR 1.0; non-contrast CT: no haemorrhage, early subtle hypodensity left insula; CTA: left MCA M1 occlusion; CTP: core 15 mL, penumbra 120 mL

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management strategy?

options.text: Administer IV alteplase and arrange retrieval for thrombectomy
options.rationale: IV alteplase (thrombolysis) should be given if within the 4.5-hour window, but current Australian Stroke Foundation guidelines recommend tenecteplase as the preferred thrombolytic for large vessel occlusion stroke due to its single-bolus administration, higher recanalization rate, and logistical advantages. However, the key point is that thrombolysis should not delay transfer for thrombectomy. (Source: Stroke Foundation: Clinical Guidelines)

options.text: Administer antiplatelet therapy and manage conservatively locally
options.rationale: Conservative management with antiplatelets alone would result in a poor outcome for this patient with a large vessel occlusion and NIHSS of 18. The CT perfusion shows a large salvageable penumbra (120 mL) with a small core (15 mL), indicating significant tissue at risk that could be saved with reperfusion. Thrombectomy is strongly indicated. (Source: Stroke Foundation: Clinical Guidelines)

options.text: Administer IV heparin infusion and arrange cardiology review
options.rationale: IV heparin is not indicated in acute ischaemic stroke management. Anticoagulation for the underlying atrial fibrillation will be commenced after the acute phase (typically 4-14 days post-stroke depending on infarct size). The immediate priority is reperfusion therapy: thrombolysis plus thrombectomy for this large vessel occlusion. (Source: Stroke Foundation: Clinical Guidelines)

options.text: Reduce BP to <140/90 first, then consider thrombolysis and transfer
options.rationale: The BP target before thrombolysis is <185/110 mmHg, not <140/90. This patient's BP of 172/94 is already below the thrombolysis threshold and does not require reduction. Aggressive BP lowering to <140/90 in acute ischaemic stroke may worsen ischaemia by reducing perfusion to the penumbra. Time to treatment is critical and should not be delayed for unnecessary BP management. (Source: Stroke Foundation: Clinical Guidelines)

options.text: Give IV tenecteplase and arrange urgent transfer for thrombectomy
options.rationale: This patient meets criteria for both thrombolysis (within 4.5 hours, no contraindications) and thrombectomy (large vessel occlusion, NIHSS >=6, small core with large penumbra mismatch). Australian guidelines now recommend tenecteplase over alteplase for large vessel occlusion. The drip-and-ship model (thrombolyse locally, transfer for thrombectomy) is appropriate when the nearest comprehensive stroke centre is hours away. Transfer should not be delayed for post-thrombolysis observation. (Source: Stroke Foundation: Clinical Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Acute ischaemic stroke with large vessel occlusion requires a combined approach: IV thrombolysis (tenecteplase preferred for LVO in Australia) at the presenting hospital followed by urgent transfer for endovascular thrombectomy. The CT perfusion mismatch (small core, large penumbra) confirms salvageable tissue. Thrombectomy has been shown to improve outcomes up to 24 hours from onset when perfusion imaging demonstrates a target mismatch. The drip-and-ship model enables thrombolysis at regional centres with transfer to comprehensive stroke centres for thrombectomy.
key_points: Tenecteplase is now preferred over alteplase for large vessel occlusion stroke in Australia
Thrombectomy is indicated for LVO with NIHSS >=6 and favourable perfusion imaging
Drip-and-ship model: thrombolyse locally, transfer urgently for thrombectomy
BP target for thrombolysis eligibility is <185/110 mmHg, not <140/90
CT perfusion mismatch (small core, large penumbra) identifies patients who benefit from thrombectomy up to 24 hours
explanation.pearls: The EXTEND-IA TNK trial conducted in Australia demonstrated that tenecteplase achieved higher rates of MCA reperfusion before thrombectomy than alteplase (22% vs 10%), supporting its use as the preferred thrombolytic in the drip-and-ship pathway.

stem: A 66-year-old man received IV tenecteplase 45 minutes ago for an acute left MCA territory ischaemic stroke with an NIHSS of 12. He is not a candidate for thrombectomy. He has been admitted to the stroke unit. His neurological status is stable with no signs of haemorrhagic transformation. His BP has been fluctuating between 165-180/90-100 mmHg since admission.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 78 (regular), BP 178/96 mmHg, RR 16, SpO2 97% RA, Temp 37.0, GCS 12
data_table.Investigations: Repeat non-contrast CT at 24 hours: no haemorrhagic transformation

lead_in: What is the appropriate blood pressure target for the next 24 hours?

options.text: Maintain BP below 180/105 mmHg with IV antihypertensive PRN
options.rationale: After IV thrombolysis for ischaemic stroke, blood pressure should be maintained below 180/105 mmHg for the first 24 hours to reduce the risk of symptomatic intracranial haemorrhage. This is the standard post-thrombolysis BP target. Frequent monitoring (every 15 minutes for the first 2 hours, then every 30 minutes for 6 hours, then hourly for 16 hours) is required. IV labetalol or hydralazine are common choices for acute BP management. (Source: Stroke Foundation: Clinical Guidelines)

options.text: Reduce BP to below 140/90 mmHg immediately with IV infusion
options.rationale: Aggressive BP reduction to <140/90 in the first 24 hours post-thrombolysis is not recommended and may worsen outcomes. The ischaemic penumbra relies on collateral perfusion, which is pressure-dependent. Rapid lowering below 140/90 may extend the infarct. The ENCHANTED trial showed no benefit of intensive BP lowering to <140 mmHg systolic after thrombolysis. (Source: Stroke Foundation: Clinical Guidelines)

options.text: Allow permissive hypertension up to 220/120 mmHg post-thrombolysis
options.rationale: Permissive hypertension up to 220/120 mmHg is the approach for ischaemic stroke patients who have NOT received thrombolysis. Once thrombolysis has been administered, the BP target is stricter (<180/105) because hypertension increases the risk of haemorrhagic transformation of the thrombolysed tissue. (Source: AHA/ASA Stroke Guidelines)

options.text: Target systolic BP of 120 to 130 mmHg with glyceryl trinitrate patch
options.rationale: GTN patches were studied in the RIGHT-2 and ENOS trials for acute stroke BP management. Neither showed benefit from acute BP lowering in the first 48 hours of stroke. A target of 120-130 mmHg systolic is too aggressive in the acute post-thrombolysis period and risks worsening ischaemia. (Source: Stroke Foundation: Clinical Guidelines)

options.text: Withhold all antihypertensives and monitor without intervention
options.rationale: While avoiding excessive BP lowering is important, completely withholding antihypertensives when BP exceeds the post-thrombolysis threshold of 180/105 is inappropriate. The current BP of 178/96 is just below the threshold and may exceed it, requiring treatment. Active monitoring with PRN antihypertensive to maintain <180/105 is the correct approach. (Source: AHA/ASA Stroke Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Blood pressure management after thrombolysis for ischaemic stroke follows strict protocols. The target is <180/105 mmHg for the first 24 hours to minimise haemorrhagic transformation risk while maintaining adequate cerebral perfusion. This is stricter than the non-thrombolysed target (<220/120). Frequent BP monitoring is essential. After 24 hours, gradual introduction of oral antihypertensives is appropriate. Long-term BP control to <130/80 reduces recurrent stroke risk.
key_points: Post-thrombolysis BP target: <180/105 mmHg for the first 24 hours
Non-thrombolysed ischaemic stroke: permissive hypertension up to 220/120 mmHg
Frequent monitoring: every 15 min for 2 hours, then every 30 min for 6 hours, then hourly
IV labetalol, hydralazine, or glyceryl trinitrate infusion for acute BP management
After 24 hours without haemorrhage, gradually commence oral antihypertensives
explanation.pearls: The BP target after thrombolysis (<180/105) is 40 mmHg systolic lower than the non-thrombolysed target (<220/120) - this reflects the increased haemorrhagic transformation risk after thrombolysis, which rises approximately 6% for every 10 mmHg increase in systolic BP above 180.

stem: A 62-year-old woman with recently diagnosed metastatic pancreatic adenocarcinoma on gemcitabine-based chemotherapy develops acute left leg swelling. Compression ultrasound confirms an extensive iliofemoral deep vein thrombosis. She has normal renal function and no thrombocytopenia. She has no history of gastrointestinal bleeding. Her oncologist confirms she will continue chemotherapy.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 92, BP 122/76 mmHg, RR 18, SpO2 97% RA
data_table.Investigations: FBC: Hb 108 g/L, WCC 5.2 x10^9/L, platelets 185 x10^9/L; UEC: eGFR >90; LFTs: mild cholestasis; coags: INR 1.1

lead_in: What is the most appropriate anticoagulation strategy?

options.text: Warfarin with a target INR of 2.0 to 3.0 with LMWH bridging
options.rationale: Warfarin is inferior to both LMWH and DOACs for cancer-associated VTE. The CLOT trial demonstrated that dalteparin reduced VTE recurrence by 52% compared to warfarin in cancer patients. Warfarin also has significant drug interactions with chemotherapy agents and variable absorption in patients with GI malignancies or mucositis. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular)

options.text: Apixaban 10 mg BD for 7 days then 5 mg BD for at least 6 months
options.rationale: DOACs (apixaban and rivaroxaban specifically) are now recommended first-line for cancer-associated VTE in patients without high GI bleeding risk. The Caravaggio trial demonstrated non-inferiority of apixaban to dalteparin for cancer-associated VTE with similar major bleeding rates. Pancreatic cancer was not associated with excess GI bleeding with apixaban in this trial. Treatment should continue for at least 6 months and as long as cancer is active or chemotherapy continues. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular)

options.text: Subcutaneous enoxaparin 1.5 mg/kg daily as the preferred option
options.rationale: LMWH (enoxaparin or dalteparin) was the previous standard of care for cancer-associated VTE. However, the SELECT-D and Caravaggio trials demonstrated that DOACs (rivaroxaban and apixaban) are non-inferior with comparable safety. DOACs are preferred for patient convenience (oral administration, no injections) unless there is a high GI bleeding risk from a luminal GI malignancy. (Source: ISTH: Cancer-Associated VTE)

options.text: Rivaroxaban 15 mg BD for 21 days then 20 mg daily for six months
options.rationale: Rivaroxaban is an acceptable DOAC for cancer-associated VTE. However, the SELECT-D trial showed a higher rate of clinically relevant non-major bleeding with rivaroxaban compared to dalteparin, particularly in upper GI malignancies. For pancreatic cancer, apixaban (Caravaggio trial data) demonstrated a more favourable safety profile. Both are reasonable but apixaban is generally preferred. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular)

options.text: Insert an IVC filter and defer anticoagulation due to cancer risk
options.rationale: IVC filters are reserved for patients with acute VTE who have absolute contraindications to anticoagulation (active major bleeding, very severe thrombocytopenia). This patient has no contraindication to anticoagulation. IVC filters do not treat existing DVT, carry their own thrombotic complications, and should be retrieved once anticoagulation can be commenced. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular)

explanation.summary: Cancer-associated VTE is treated with anticoagulation for at least 6 months and continued as long as cancer is active or treatment is ongoing. DOACs (apixaban and rivaroxaban) are now first-line for most cancer-associated VTE, replacing LMWH as the standard of care. The exception is high GI bleeding risk from luminal GI malignancies (oesophageal, gastric, small bowel, colorectal) where LMWH may be preferred. Warfarin is inferior to both DOACs and LMWH in cancer patients.
key_points: DOACs (apixaban, rivaroxaban) are first-line for most cancer-associated VTE
LMWH is preferred over DOACs for luminal GI malignancies with high GI bleeding risk
Treatment duration: at least 6 months, continued while cancer is active or on treatment
Warfarin is inferior to both DOACs and LMWH for cancer-associated VTE
IVC filters are only for patients with absolute contraindications to anticoagulation
explanation.pearls: The Caravaggio trial specifically included pancreatic cancer patients and showed no excess GI bleeding with apixaban vs dalteparin - this distinguishes it from the SELECT-D trial where rivaroxaban showed higher bleeding in upper GI cancers, making apixaban the generally preferred DOAC for cancer-associated VTE.

stem: A 58-year-old man presents with a 4-day history of productive cough with rust-coloured sputum, fevers, and pleuritic chest pain. He has no significant comorbidities and takes no regular medications. He is alert and oriented. A chest X-ray shows right lower lobe consolidation. He has no drug allergies.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 98, BP 128/78 mmHg, RR 22, SpO2 94% RA, Temp 38.8
data_table.Investigations: FBC: WCC 16.2 x10^9/L; CRP 185 mg/L; UEC: urea 4.2 mmol/L, creatinine 85 micromol/L; albumin 34 g/L; ABG on RA: pH 7.42, pO2 68 mmHg, pCO2 36 mmHg

lead_in: What is the most appropriate empiric antibiotic regimen?

options.text: Oral amoxicillin 1 g TDS for 5 days as outpatient treatment
options.rationale: Oral amoxicillin monotherapy is appropriate for low-severity CAP (SMART-COP 0-2) managed in the community. This patient has moderate-severity pneumonia: SpO2 94%, respiratory rate 22, and PaO2 68 mmHg suggest he is borderline for hospital admission. SMART-COP scoring indicates he may benefit from inpatient management with broader empiric coverage. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: IV ceftriaxone 1 g daily and oral azithromycin 500 mg daily
options.rationale: IV ceftriaxone plus a macrolide is appropriate for severe CAP requiring ICU admission or SMART-COP score >=5 (high risk of requiring intensive respiratory or vasopressor support). This patient's presentation is moderate, not severe - his SMART-COP score is approximately 2-3 (SpO2 <=93 earns 2 points, RR >=25 would earn 1). Moderate CAP does not routinely require IV combination therapy. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Oral amoxicillin 1 g TDS and oral doxycycline 100 mg BD for 5 days
options.rationale: For moderate-severity CAP requiring hospital admission (SMART-COP 3-4), Australian Therapeutic Guidelines recommend oral amoxicillin 1 g TDS plus doxycycline 100 mg BD (or a macrolide) to cover both typical (Streptococcus pneumoniae) and atypical (Mycoplasma, Legionella) organisms. This patient's SpO2 of 94% and PaO2 of 68 mmHg warrant admission but not ICU-level therapy. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: IV piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 g TDS for broad Gram-negative cover
options.rationale: Piperacillin-tazobactam provides broad Gram-negative and anaerobic cover and is not first-line for community-acquired pneumonia. It is reserved for hospital-acquired pneumonia, aspiration pneumonia with significant anaerobic risk, or when Pseudomonas coverage is specifically required (e.g. structural lung disease, recent hospitalisation). (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Oral ciprofloxacin 500 mg BD for 7 days as fluoroquinolone cover
options.rationale: Respiratory fluoroquinolones (moxifloxacin, levofloxacin) have a role in CAP in some international guidelines, but ciprofloxacin has poor pneumococcal coverage and is not appropriate for CAP. Australian guidelines do not recommend fluoroquinolones as first-line for CAP due to resistance concerns and the availability of effective alternatives. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

explanation.summary: Australian CAP management uses the SMART-COP tool to stratify severity and guide antibiotic choice and disposition. SMART-COP predicts the need for intensive respiratory or vasopressor support. Low-severity (0-2 points): oral amoxicillin, outpatient. Moderate (3-4): oral amoxicillin plus doxycycline, hospital ward. Severe (>=5): IV ceftriaxone plus azithromycin, consider ICU. SMART-COP parameters include systolic BP, multilobar involvement, albumin, respiratory rate, tachycardia, confusion, oxygen, and pH.
key_points: SMART-COP stratifies CAP severity: 0-2 mild, 3-4 moderate, >=5 severe
Moderate CAP: oral amoxicillin 1 g TDS plus doxycycline 100 mg BD (or azithromycin)
Severe CAP: IV ceftriaxone 1 g daily plus azithromycin 500 mg daily
Duration of antibiotics for uncomplicated CAP is 5 days (review at 48-72 hours)
Atypical cover (doxycycline or macrolide) is added for moderate and severe CAP
explanation.pearls: SMART-COP was developed in Australia specifically for CAP and predicts ICU admission better than CURB-65 or PSI - the oxygen component (PaO2 <70 mmHg or SpO2 <=93% on room air) earns 2 points and is the most heavily weighted parameter.

subtopic_detail: Acute cerebellar ataxia vs posterior fossa tumour
stem: A 3-year-old boy presents with a 2-day history of progressive unsteadiness and frequent falls. He had chickenpox (varicella) 10 days ago that has resolved. His parents report he was walking normally before this. On examination he has truncal ataxia, wide-based gait, and bilateral dysmetria. His speech is normal and there is no nystagmus. He is afebrile, alert, and interactive. There is no papilloedema on fundoscopy. He has no headache or vomiting.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 105, BP 95/60 mmHg, RR 22, SpO2 99% RA, Temp 36.8
data_table.Examination: Truncal ataxia, wide-based gait, bilateral dysmetria on finger-nose, no nystagmus, no papilloedema, no cranial nerve deficit, normal tone and power in all limbs

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Diagnose acute cerebellar ataxia and discharge with GP follow-up
options.rationale: While post-infectious (post-varicella) acute cerebellar ataxia is the most likely diagnosis given the recent chickenpox and absence of red flag features, it is a diagnosis of exclusion. An MRI brain must be performed to exclude a posterior fossa tumour before this diagnosis can be made with confidence. Posterior fossa tumours can present identically in this age group. (Source: RCH CPG: Ataxia)

options.text: Commence IV aciclovir for possible varicella cerebellitis urgently
options.rationale: Post-varicella cerebellar ataxia is an immune-mediated process, not active viral infection of the cerebellum. It occurs 1-2 weeks after the primary infection and is thought to result from cross-reactive antibodies or T-cell responses against cerebellar antigens. Aciclovir does not treat the ataxia. If varicella encephalitis were suspected (altered consciousness, seizures), aciclovir would be indicated, but this child is alert and well. (Source: RCH CPG: Ataxia)

options.text: Administer IV dexamethasone for presumed cerebellar inflammation
options.rationale: Corticosteroids are not routinely recommended for acute post-infectious cerebellar ataxia, which is typically self-limiting with resolution over days to weeks. If a more severe acute disseminated encephalomyelitis (ADEM) pattern were identified on MRI, corticosteroids might be indicated. Empiric steroids before imaging could also mask or complicate interpretation of a posterior fossa tumour. (Source: RCH CPG: Ataxia)

options.text: Arrange MRI brain to exclude a posterior fossa mass lesion
options.rationale: In any child presenting with new-onset ataxia, MRI brain is essential to exclude a posterior fossa tumour (medulloblastoma, astrocytoma, ependymoma) before attributing symptoms to a benign post-infectious aetiology. Posterior fossa tumours are the most common solid tumours of childhood and present with ataxia in this age group. The absence of headache, vomiting, papilloedema, and cranial nerve signs makes tumour less likely but does not exclude it. (Source: RCH CPG: Ataxia)

options.text: Perform lumbar puncture to exclude meningitis or encephalitis
options.rationale: The child is afebrile, alert, interactive, and has no meningism or encephalopathic features. Lumbar puncture is not the priority investigation. While CSF analysis can support a diagnosis of post-infectious cerebellitis (mild pleocytosis), the critical investigation is MRI to exclude a structural cause. LP may be considered after imaging if the clinical picture is unclear. (Source: RCH CPG: Ataxia)

explanation.summary: New-onset ataxia in a child requires neuroimaging to exclude posterior fossa tumours, which are the most common solid tumours of childhood. Post-varicella acute cerebellar ataxia is the most common cause of acute ataxia in preschool children and typically presents 1-2 weeks after varicella infection. It is a diagnosis of exclusion - MRI must be performed before the diagnosis is made. Post-infectious cerebellar ataxia is self-limiting, with most children recovering completely within 2-4 weeks.
key_points: MRI brain is mandatory for any child with new-onset ataxia to exclude posterior fossa tumour
Post-varicella cerebellar ataxia is immune-mediated, not active infection - aciclovir is not helpful
Red flags for posterior fossa tumour: persistent headache, morning vomiting, papilloedema, cranial nerve palsies
Posterior fossa tumours (medulloblastoma, astrocytoma, ependymoma) are the most common solid tumours of childhood
Post-infectious cerebellar ataxia is self-limiting with recovery in 2-4 weeks in most cases
explanation.pearls: Post-varicella cerebellar ataxia occurs in approximately 1 in 4,000 cases of childhood varicella and typically appears 1-2 weeks after the rash - the incidence has declined significantly since routine varicella vaccination was added to the Australian National Immunisation Program in 2005.

stem: A 62-year-old man with severe COPD (FEV1 35% predicted) on home oxygen presents with sudden worsening of his baseline dyspnoea and right-sided pleuritic chest pain over the past 2 hours. He has a 45-pack-year smoking history and is on maximal inhaled therapy. On examination, breath sounds are markedly reduced on the right with hyperresonance to percussion. His chest X-ray confirms a moderate right-sided pneumothorax with no mediastinal shift.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 110, BP 118/72 mmHg, RR 28, SpO2 85% on 2 L/min nasal prong oxygen (baseline SpO2 91% on 2 L), Temp 36.6
data_table.Investigations: Chest X-ray: moderate right pneumothorax, bilateral emphysematous changes, no tension

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial management?

options.text: Attempt needle aspiration as first-line as per primary pneumothorax
options.rationale: Needle aspiration is first-line for primary spontaneous pneumothorax but is not recommended for secondary pneumothorax. The success rate of aspiration in secondary pneumothorax is significantly lower (approximately 30-40% vs 70-80% in primary) due to the underlying lung disease preventing re-expansion. Guidelines recommend intercostal drain as first-line for secondary pneumothorax. (Source: BTS: Pneumothorax Guidelines)

options.text: Observe with supplemental oxygen and repeat imaging in 6 hours
options.rationale: Observation may be considered for very small secondary pneumothorax (<1 cm) in minimally symptomatic patients, but this patient is significantly more symptomatic than baseline (SpO2 dropped from 91% to 85%, tachycardic, tachypnoeic). A moderate pneumothorax in severe COPD is poorly tolerated and requires active drainage. (Source: BTS: Pneumothorax Guidelines)

options.text: Increase supplemental oxygen to 15 L/min via non-rebreather mask
options.rationale: While supplemental oxygen assists pneumothorax reabsorption and treats hypoxia, high-flow oxygen alone does not address a moderate secondary pneumothorax in a patient with severe COPD. There is also a risk of hypercapnic respiratory failure with excessive oxygen in COPD patients. Active drainage is required alongside titrated oxygen therapy. (Source: BTS: Pneumothorax Guidelines)

options.text: Arrange urgent CT thorax before any intervention to plan approach
options.rationale: CT can help differentiate pneumothorax from large bullae in COPD patients, which is an important distinction. However, in this case the diagnosis is clear on chest X-ray, and the patient is acutely deteriorating. Delaying drainage for CT imaging risks further deterioration. CT may be performed after initial stabilisation if there is diagnostic uncertainty. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Respiratory)

options.text: Insert a small-bore intercostal catheter connected to underwater seal
options.rationale: Secondary spontaneous pneumothorax in a patient with significant underlying lung disease requires intercostal drain insertion as first-line management. A small-bore catheter (10-14F) connected to an underwater seal drain is recommended. This patient has severe COPD with limited respiratory reserve, and the pneumothorax has caused significant desaturation below his baseline. Admission, drainage, and respiratory monitoring are essential. Suction may be added if the lung fails to re-expand. (Source: BTS: Pneumothorax Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Secondary spontaneous pneumothorax occurs in patients with underlying lung disease (COPD, CF, ILD) and is more dangerous than primary pneumothorax due to limited respiratory reserve. Management differs from primary pneumothorax: intercostal drain insertion is first-line rather than needle aspiration, as aspiration has a low success rate in diseased lungs. All patients with secondary pneumothorax should be admitted. Referral for surgical pleurodesis should be considered after first episode given the high recurrence rate (40-50%).
key_points: Intercostal drain is first-line for secondary pneumothorax (not needle aspiration)
Secondary pneumothorax is more dangerous than primary due to limited respiratory reserve
All secondary pneumothorax patients should be admitted to hospital
Small-bore drains (10-14F) are preferred over large-bore
Recurrence rate is 40-50% - early surgical referral for pleurodesis should be considered after first episode
explanation.pearls: Large emphysematous bullae on CT can mimic pneumothorax on plain X-ray in COPD patients - inserting a drain into a bulla is dangerous; if there is any diagnostic uncertainty, CT should be obtained before intervention.
